title: SQL Introduction
content:
SQL Introduction

What is SQL?

SQL stands for Structured Query Language. It is the standard language used to manage, manipulate, and query data in relational databases.



  • Insert new data
  • Update existing data
  • Delete data
  • Retrieve (Query) data

title: SQL Syntax
content:
SQL Syntax

Basic Rules

SQL statements are not case-sensitive (select is the same as SELECT), but it is a best practice to capitalize SQL keywords for readability. Each SQL statement should end with a semicolon (;).

Syntax Example
SELECT * FROM Customers;

title: SQL SELECT
content:
SQL SELECT Statement

Retrieving Data

The SELECT statement is used to select data from a database. You can select specific columns, or use the asterisk (*) to select all columns.

SELECT Examples
-- Select specific columns
SELECT CustomerName, Country FROM Customers;

-- Select all columns
SELECT * FROM Customers;

title: SQL WHERE
content:
SQL WHERE Clause

Filtering Data

The WHERE clause is used to filter records. It extracts only those records that fulfill a specified condition.

WHERE Example
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Mexico';

title: SQL ORDER BY
content:
SQL ORDER BY

Sorting Results

The ORDER BY keyword is used to sort the result-set in ascending (ASC) or descending (DESC) order. By default, it sorts in ascending order.

Sorting Example
-- Sort alphabetically by Name
SELECT * FROM Customers
ORDER BY CustomerName ASC;

-- Sort backwards
SELECT * FROM Customers
ORDER BY CustomerName DESC;

title: SQL INSERT INTO
content:
SQL INSERT INTO

Adding New Records

The INSERT INTO statement is used to add new rows of data to a table.

INSERT Example
INSERT INTO Customers (CustomerID, CustomerName, ContactName, Country)
VALUES (6, 'Sanjith', 'Sanjith', 'India');

-- Run this, then change the code to 'SELECT * FROM Customers;' to see your new row!

title: SQL UPDATE
content:
SQL UPDATE

Modifying Records

The UPDATE statement is used to modify existing records in a table. Warning: Always use a WHERE clause! If you forget it, you will update EVERY record in the table!

UPDATE Example
UPDATE Customers
SET Country = 'USA'
WHERE CustomerName = 'Alfreds Futterkiste';

title: SQL DELETE
content:
SQL DELETE

Removing Records

The DELETE statement is used to delete existing records in a table. Just like UPDATE, be careful to always include your WHERE clause!

DELETE Example
DELETE FROM Customers
WHERE CustomerName = 'Berglunds snabbköp';

title: SQL LIMIT
content:
SQL LIMIT

Restricting Results

The LIMIT clause is used to specify the maximum number of records to return. This is highly useful on large tables with thousands of records to prevent the database from crashing.

LIMIT Example
SELECT * FROM Customers
LIMIT 3;

title: Aggregate Functions
content:
SQL Aggregate Functions

Performing Calculations

Aggregate functions perform a calculation on a set of values and return a single value.

  • COUNT() - Returns the number of rows
  • SUM() - Returns the total sum of a numeric column
  • AVG() - Returns the average value
  • MIN() & MAX() - Return the smallest/largest value
COUNT Example
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Customers;

title: SQL GROUP BY
content:
SQL GROUP BY

Grouping Data

The GROUP BY statement groups rows that have the same values into summary rows. It is often used with aggregate functions to group the result-set by one or more columns.

GROUP BY Example
-- Count how many customers live in each country
SELECT Country, COUNT(*)
FROM Customers
GROUP BY Country;

title: SQL HAVING
content:
SQL HAVING

Filtering Groups

The HAVING clause was added to SQL because the WHERE keyword cannot be used with aggregate functions. It filters the results *after* they have been grouped.

HAVING Example
-- Show only countries that have more than 1 customer
SELECT Country, COUNT(*)
FROM Customers
GROUP BY Country
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

title: SQL JOIN
content:
SQL JOIN

Connecting Tables

A JOIN clause is used to combine rows from two or more tables, based on a related column between them (usually a Primary Key and a Foreign Key).



INNER JOIN Example
-- Connect the Orders table to the Customers table!
SELECT Orders.OrderID, Customers.CustomerName
FROM Orders
INNER JOIN Customers
ON Orders.CustomerID = Customers.CustomerID;

title: CREATE TABLE
content:
SQL CREATE TABLE

Building Structure

The CREATE TABLE statement is used to create a new table in a database. You must define the name of the column and the data type (INT, VARCHAR, etc.).

CREATE TABLE Example
CREATE TABLE Persons (
  ID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Name VARCHAR(100),
  Country VARCHAR(50)
);

-- Run this, then change the code to 'SELECT * FROM Persons;' to see your empty table!

title: DROP TABLE
content:
SQL DROP TABLE

Deleting Structure

The DROP TABLE statement is used to completely destroy an existing table in a database, erasing both its structure and all of its data. Be very careful!

DROP TABLE Example
-- This will delete the table we just created in the previous step!
DROP TABLE Persons;

title: SQL Constraints
content:
SQL Constraints

Enforcing Rules

Constraints are rules enforced on data columns in a table. They limit the type of data that can go into a table, ensuring accuracy and reliability.

  • NOT NULL: Column cannot have a NULL value.
  • UNIQUE: All values in a column must be different.
  • CHECK: Ensures all values satisfy a specific condition.
  • DEFAULT: Sets a default value if none is provided.
Constraints Example
CREATE TABLE Users (
  ID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  Age INT CHECK (Age >= 18)
);

-- Try inserting a user who is 15. The database will reject it!

title: PRIMARY KEY
content:
SQL PRIMARY KEY

Unique Identifiers

The PRIMARY KEY constraint uniquely identifies each record in a table. Primary keys must contain UNIQUE values, and cannot contain NULL values. A table can have only ONE primary key.



Primary Key Syntax
CREATE TABLE Students (
  StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  Name VARCHAR(50)
);

INSERT INTO Students (StudentID, Name) VALUES (1, 'Sanjith');
-- If you try to insert another student with StudentID 1, it will fail!

title: FOREIGN KEY
content:
SQL FOREIGN KEY

Linking Tables

A FOREIGN KEY is a field (or collection of fields) in one table that refers to the PRIMARY KEY in another table. It is used to prevent actions that would destroy links between tables.



Foreign Key Example
CREATE TABLE Invoices (
  InvoiceID INT PRIMARY KEY,
  CustomerID INT,
  Amount DECIMAL(10, 2),
  FOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customers(CustomerID)
);

title: SQL ALTER TABLE
content:
SQL ALTER TABLE

Modifying Structure

The ALTER TABLE statement is used to add, delete, or modify columns in an existing table.

ALTER Examples
-- Add a new column
ALTER TABLE Customers
ADD Email VARCHAR(100);

-- Drop an existing column
ALTER TABLE Customers
DROP COLUMN ContactName;

title: SQL DISTINCT
content:
SQL DISTINCT

Unique Values Only

The SELECT DISTINCT statement is used to return only distinct (different) values. Inside a table, a column often contains many duplicate values; and sometimes you only want to list the different ones.

DISTINCT Example
-- This will list each country only once, even if 50 customers live there!
SELECT DISTINCT Country FROM Customers;

title: SQL BETWEEN
content:
SQL BETWEEN

Selecting Ranges

The BETWEEN operator selects values within a given range. The values can be numbers, text, or dates. The BETWEEN operator is inclusive: begin and end values are included.

BETWEEN Example
SELECT * FROM Orders
WHERE OrderID BETWEEN 10248 AND 10250;

title: SQL LIKE
content:
SQL LIKE

Pattern Matching

The LIKE operator is used in a WHERE clause to search for a specified pattern in a column. The percent sign (%) represents zero, one, or multiple characters.

LIKE Example
-- Find all customers whose name starts with the letter 'A'
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE CustomerName LIKE 'A%';

title: SQL IN
content:
SQL IN Operator

Multiple Conditions

The IN operator allows you to specify multiple values in a WHERE clause. It is a shorthand for multiple OR conditions.

IN Example
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country IN ('Mexico', 'Germany', 'UK');

title: SQL NULL
content:
SQL NULL Values

Handling Empty Data

A field with a NULL value is a field with no value. It is very important to understand that a NULL value is different from a zero value or a field that contains spaces. Always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL to look for them!

NULL Check Example
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE ContactName IS NULL;

title: SQL Views
content:
SQL Views

Virtual Tables

In SQL, a view is a virtual table based on the result-set of an SQL statement. A view contains rows and columns, just like a real table. The fields in a view are fields from one or more real tables in the database.



View Example
CREATE VIEW MexicoCustomers AS
SELECT CustomerName, ContactName
FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Mexico';

-- Now you can query the View just like a normal table!
SELECT * FROM MexicoCustomers;

title: SQL Index
content:
SQL Index

Speeding Up Queries

Indexes are used to retrieve data from the database more quickly than otherwise. The users cannot see the indexes, they are just used to speed up searches/queries. Note: Updating a table with indexes takes more time, so only create indexes on columns that will be frequently searched against.



Index Example
CREATE INDEX idx_customer_name
ON Customers (CustomerName);

title: Stored Procedures
content:
SQL Stored Procedures

Reusable Code

A stored procedure is a prepared SQL code that you can save, so the code can be reused over and over again.

Note: Stored Procedures are heavily used in enterprise databases like SQL Server and MySQL, but are not supported in lightweight, browser-based database engines like SQLite (which powers our Live Editor).

Procedure Example (SQL Server)
-- This syntax will not run in our browser simulator, but is great to know!
CREATE PROCEDURE SelectAllCustomers
AS
SELECT * FROM Customers;
GO;

EXEC SelectAllCustomers;

title: SQL Triggers
content:
SQL Triggers

Automatic Actions

A trigger is a special type of stored procedure that automatically runs when an event occurs in the database server (like an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE).

Trigger Example (SQLite)
-- Creates a log table, then sets a trigger to log whenever a customer is deleted!
CREATE TABLE Logs (LogMessage TEXT);

CREATE TRIGGER trgAfterDelete
AFTER DELETE ON Customers
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO Logs (LogMessage) VALUES ('A customer was deleted!');
END;

-- Try running this, then DELETE a customer, then SELECT * FROM Logs!

title: SQL Transactions
content:
SQL Transactions

Data Integrity (ACID)

A transaction is a unit of work that is performed against a database. Transactions ensure that either all the commands in the block succeed, or none of them do (like transferring money between bank accounts). If something fails, you can ROLLBACK to the previous state. If it succeeds, you COMMIT.



Transaction Example
BEGIN TRANSACTION;

UPDATE Customers 
SET ContactName = 'Transaction Test' 
WHERE CustomerID = 1;

COMMIT;

title: Course Summary
content:
SQL Course Summary

Database Mastery Achieved! 🎉

You have successfully completed the SQL course. You now have the skills to build, manipulate, and query complex relational databases.

  • Basic Queries: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY
  • Manipulating Data: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
  • Advanced Concepts: JOINs, Grouping, Views, and Transactions

You are now ready to tackle advanced database architecture and backend engineering!

title: SQL Subqueries
content:
SQL Subqueries

Nested Queries

A subquery is a query nested inside another query. They are excellent for breaking down complex SQL problems into logical steps.

Subquery Example
SELECT CustomerName FROM Customers
WHERE CustomerID IN (SELECT CustomerID FROM Orders);
